acceptance: An upgraded Postgres database containing a legacy schema row stored under the raw qualified collection string is fully cleaned up by unregister_collection; a regression test seeds that legacy row and verifies no schema metadata remains after qualified unregister.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:215
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:1180
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:2166
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: ensure_namespace_catalog_tables backfills only database_name and schema_name for existing schema rows by joining on s.collection = c.name, but it never rewrites legacy schema rows whose collection column still contains the raw qualified identifier. The reviewed change makes unregister_collection delete schema rows only under the normalized bare key (key.collection). On an upgraded database that still has pre-fix schema metadata keyed as prod.billing.invoices, calling unregister_collection(prod.billing.invoices) deletes the normalized collection row but leaves those legacy schema-history rows orphaned. The new regression test covers only normalized rows, so this upgrade path remains unverified.
Suggested fix: Extend the upgrade or unregister cleanup path to remove legacy schema rows keyed by the raw qualified collection string in addition to the normalized bare key, and add a regression test that seeds a pre-fix schema row before running qualified unregister on an upgraded adapter.
title: correctness: upgraded Postgres schema metadata can survive qualified unregister

acceptance: A regression test covers a shape-compatible helper contract that uses non-EntityRecord type names, and fetchEntity()/fetchEntities() accept the contract when the required fields and arguments are present; the targeted probe still avoids the full __schema.types scan.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: ui/src/lib/graphql.ts:90
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The narrowed helper-contract probe now assumes the backend exposes the helper object types under the exact names EntityRecord, EntityConnection, and EntityEdge. That assumption is not part of the helper query contract: GraphQL selection sets depend on field shape, and FEAT-015 / ADR-012 only standardize CollectionMeta, EntityConnection, EntityEdge, and PageInfo for the generic schema, not an EntityRecord type. The old implementation resolved the actual return type name from __schema.types, but the new switch in getNamedType() only returns metadata for the hard-coded aliases. Reproduced by stubbing /graphql so entity(collection, id) returns a shape-compatible TaskRecord and entities(...) returns TaskConnection; fetchEntity() now rejects with "Axon /graphql does not expose the entity helper contract yet" after a single probe call even though the queried fields are present.
Suggested fix: Keep the targeted probe, but resolve named types generically from the probed field metadata instead of keying on hard-coded aliases. For example, fetch the specific type names discovered on the root fields (or preserve a small name-to-metadata map) so helper support is validated by field shape rather than exact type names.
title: correctness: GraphQL helper probe hard-codes helper type names

acceptance: Force-dropping a namespace or database removes entity rows only for collections inside the dropped scope, same-named collections in surviving namespaces/databases retain their entities, and regression tests cover Memory, SQLite, and Postgres shared-name scenarios.
description:

File: crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs:790
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:580
Category: integration
Severity: high
Description: The new purge paths delete by bare collection name, but all three storage adapters still key entity storage by bare collection name as well. FEAT-014 explicitly allows the same collection name to exist in multiple namespaces and databases. As a result, force-dropping prod.billing.invoices can also delete surviving data in prod.engineering.invoices or default.default.invoices. The existing same-name tests only check catalog membership, so this regression is currently untested.
Suggested fix: Scope entity storage and purge operations by fully qualified collection identity (database/schema + collection, or collection numeric ID), and add regression tests proving force-drop preserves same-named collections in surviving namespaces/databases across Memory, SQLite, and Postgres.
title: integration: force-drop purges same-named collections outside the target namespace/database

acceptance: Calling unregister_collection with a qualified collection ID removes the Postgres collections, schemas, and collection_views rows stored under the resolved bare collection key for that namespace; a regression test covers qualified unregister after schema and collection-view writes.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:1168
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:1180
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:1192
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: This commit normalizes Postgres schema and collection-view rows to the resolved bare collection key (key.collection), but unregister_collection still deletes collection_views, schemas, and collections using the caller's raw collection string. A qualified unregister such as unregister_collection(prod.billing.invoices) resolves the namespace correctly, then issues WHERE collection = 'prod.billing.invoices' even though the persisted rows now use invoices. The delete statements become no-ops, leaving the registry row and metadata behind.
Suggested fix: Use the resolved catalog key consistently in unregister_collection (key.collection for collection/name) and add a regression test that unregistering a uniquely resolvable qualified collection removes the collection row plus associated schema/view metadata.
title: correctness: unregister_collection still targets raw qualified metadata keys

acceptance: Dropping a collection removes any stored collection view in every storage adapter. Recreating the same collection starts with no collection view and versioning from 1. A regression test covers drop+recreate behavior.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:1160
Category: integration
Severity: high
Description: drop_collection deletes entities, schema, and the collection registry entry, but never calls delete_collection_view. The new collection_views stores introduced in this commit therefore retain templates after a collection is dropped. If the same collection name is recreated later, the stale template and incremented view version silently reappear, which violates collection lifecycle expectations and leaves orphaned presentation state behind.
Suggested fix: Delete the collection view as part of the drop path and add an integration test that creates a view, drops the collection, recreates it, and verifies no prior view remains.
title: integration: dropping a collection leaves its collection view behind

acceptance: A gateway test injects a render-failing template and asserts GET /collections/{collection}/entities/{id}?format=markdown returns 500 with both error detail and the entity JSON payload; cargo test -p axon-server passes
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:278-302
File: crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs:286-307
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The new markdown GET path renders through `AxonHandler::get_entity_markdown`, which returns only a `String`. When rendering fails, the handler maps the error into `AxonError::Storage`, and the gateway converts that into a generic 500 JSON error with no entity payload. FEAT-026 explicitly requires that if `format=markdown` rendering fails, the response still includes the entity JSON so callers retain access to the underlying data. As shipped, any invalid persisted template or other render-time failure drops the entity body entirely.
Suggested fix: Change the markdown GET flow to preserve the fetched entity on render failure and serialize it in the 500 response body alongside the error detail. Add an integration test that injects a bad stored template and asserts the 500 body includes both the error and the entity payload.
title: correctness: markdown GET 500 responses omit entity payload on render failure

acceptance: After rendering markdown through a bare collection alias, creating a same-named sibling collection, and dropping one namespace or database, any cached template associated with the dropped collection is evicted even if the bare alias is ambiguous at drop time; rendering the surviving collection via the bare alias uses the surviving collection's template; regression tests cover both namespace and database drop paths.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:142
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:1605
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:1663
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: `invalidate_markdown_templates_for_collections()` only evicts cache entries whose current `resolve_collection_key()` call succeeds. That misses entries cached under a bare collection alias when the alias becomes temporarily ambiguous before the drop. Concretely: render `notes` while only `prod.billing.notes` exists, create `prod.engineering.notes`, then drop `prod.billing`. During invalidation `resolve_collection_key("notes")` errors with "exists in multiple namespaces", so the bare `notes` cache entry is retained. After the drop, `notes` resolves to the surviving collection again and `compiled_markdown_template()` can reuse the dropped namespace's compiled template if the view version matches, returning incorrect markdown for the surviving collection. The new tests only exercise fully qualified collection IDs, so this alias path regresses silently.
Suggested fix: Evict cache entries by comparing normalized qualified keys without depending on current alias resolution success, or explicitly remove any cached alias that previously referred to a doomed collection. Add regression tests that render through a bare alias, introduce a same-named sibling collection, drop one namespace/database, and verify subsequent bare-alias markdown renders the surviving collection's template.
title: correctness: markdown cache eviction misses ambiguous bare aliases

acceptance: PUT /collections/{name}/schema accepts an optional actor field in the request body; gateway integration test asserts audit entry actor matches the provided value; existing put_schema tests continue to pass
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs:505-522
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Description: The put_schema gateway endpoint accepts Json<CollectionSchema> directly (line 508) and hardcodes actor: None when constructing PutSchemaRequest (line 518). Every other mutating endpoint uses a custom body struct with actor: Option<String> (CreateEntityBody, UpdateEntityBody, DeleteEntityBody, etc.) and passes body.actor through. This means REST API callers cannot provide audit provenance for schema updates — the audit actor will always be 'anonymous'. The handler layer supports actor (added in hx-2a25bfc6) but the HTTP layer does not pass it through.
Suggested fix: Create a PutSchemaBody wrapper struct (containing schema fields plus actor: Option<String>), use it as the Json extractor type in put_schema, and pass body.actor into PutSchemaRequest. Add a gateway integration test verifying actor is recorded in the audit entry after PUT /collections/{name}/schema.
title: integration: gateway put_schema endpoint does not pass actor to handler

acceptance: AxonHandler constructed with a pre-populated StorageAdapter (containing entities and schemas) correctly reports those collections via list_collections and describe_collection; test: create collection, drop and reconstruct AxonHandler, verify list_collections returns the collection
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:38
Category: correctness
Severity: high
Description: collections: HashSet<CollectionId> is an in-memory data structure not persisted via StorageAdapter. When using a durable backend (SQLite), entities and schemas survive restart but the collection registry does not. After restart, list_collections returns empty, describe_collection returns NotFound, and drop_collection returns NotFound — even though entities and schemas still exist in storage. FEAT-001 NFR states: Durability: Collection metadata is durable — survives process restart in both embedded and server modes.
Suggested fix: Add collection metadata persistence to the StorageAdapter trait (e.g. list_collections, register_collection, unregister_collection) and load the set on AxonHandler construction, or reconstruct from stored schemas at startup.
title: correctness: collection registry is ephemeral — not persisted via StorageAdapter

acceptance: A Postgres database created with the pre-fix collection_views schema removes any stored collection view when the collection is unregistered after upgrade. An automated regression test covers the Postgres upgrade path.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:43
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Description: The Postgres fix likewise adds the `collection_views -> collections` foreign key only inside `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS`. For databases that already have `collection_views` from the pre-fix schema, PostgreSQL leaves the existing table unchanged, so the new foreign key and `ON DELETE CASCADE` are never installed. `unregister_collection()` still deletes only from `collections`, which means upgraded Postgres deployments will retain stale collection-view rows after a collection is removed.
Suggested fix: Add an explicit Postgres migration or fallback cleanup path for upgraded databases. At minimum, `unregister_collection()` should delete from `collection_views` directly; ideally startup should ensure the foreign key exists on existing databases and an upgrade regression test should cover the path.
title: integration: Postgres upgrades keep stale collection views after unregister

File: crates/axon-storage/src/memory.rs:358
File: crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs:783
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:572
Category: integration

Description: This commit removed the `drop_collection` loop from both `drop_namespace` and `drop_database`, but the storage adapters still only delete catalog rows (`collections`, schemas/views, namespace metadata). None of the three adapters purge entity data in their namespace/database drop implementations, and the handler no longer emits per-collection `CollectionDrop` audit entries on forced drops. acceptance: Memory, SQLite, and Postgres store entity rows under namespace-qualified collection identity; the same entity ID can exist independently in `prod.billing.invoices` and `prod.engineering.invoices`; read/write/query/delete/count/index operations are isolated per namespace; regression tests prove isolation across all three adapters and the handler/API path.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-storage/src/memory.rs:90
File: crates/axon-storage/src/memory.rs:203
File: crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs:68
File: crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs:553
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:57
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:365
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:153
Category: correctness
Severity: high
Description: The catalog fix is namespace-aware, but the data plane is still keyed only by bare `CollectionId`. Memory keeps entity data in `HashMap<CollectionId, ...>`, SQLite and Postgres still use `entities(collection, id)` without database/schema columns, and handler entity CRUD/query paths accept only bare collection names. That means `prod.billing.invoices` and `prod.engineering.invoices` still cannot hold independent entity sets: reads, writes, counts, scans, deletes, indexes, and OCC all collapse onto the same bare `invoices` partition. FEAT-014 US-036 requires these collections to be distinct, so the current implementation remains incorrect beyond the catalog metadata.
Suggested fix: Thread namespace-qualified collection identity through entity CRUD and indexing end to end. Storage backends need namespace-qualified entity keys (or stable numeric IDs looked up from qualified collections), and the API surface needs a way to address non-default namespaces explicitly without falling back to bare-name ambiguity.
title: correctness: entity CRUD still collapses same-named collections across namespaces

acceptance: 1. Transaction::delete calls check_op_limit and returns Err when at MAX_OPS. 2. A test creates 100 delete ops then asserts the 101st delete returns InvalidArgument. 3. cargo test -p axon-api passes.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/transaction.rs:129
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: Transaction::delete does not call check_op_limit() before pushing a WriteOp, unlike create (line 91) and update (line 113). The docstring at line 128 claims it returns Err(InvalidArgument) if the transaction already has MAX_OPS operations, but the implementation never enforces this. Delete operations bypass the MAX_OPS=100 safety guard, allowing transactions to exceed the limit.
Suggested fix: Add self.check_op_limit()?; as the first statement in the delete method body, before constructing the sentinel entity.
title: correctness: Transaction::delete missing check_op_limit guard

acceptance: put_schema() with an invalid entity_schema (e.g. {"type": "bogus"}) returns AxonError::SchemaValidation; existing tests continue to pass
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:52-53,69-72
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The public put_schema() method (line 52) and the deprecated register_schema() method (line 69) on AxonHandler write schemas directly to storage without calling compile_entity_schema(). While handle_put_schema (the HTTP-facing handler) now validates, these helper methods allow callers to persist an invalid entity_schema that would cause confusing validation errors on subsequent entity writes. This is inconsistent with Principle P5 (Schema Earns Its Keep) which requires validation on every write path.
Suggested fix: Add compile_entity_schema() validation to put_schema(). For register_schema(), either add the same validation or remove the method if it is truly deprecated.
title: correctness: put_schema and register_schema bypass entity_schema validation

description:

File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:330

File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:1263
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:7249
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Description: The new template CRUD methods gate on ensure_collection_exists, which checks the requested identifier against storage.list_collections(). Every storage adapter returns only bare collection names there, while collection views and markdown rendering already support qualified identifiers like prod.billing.tasks. As a result, a registered non-default collection can successfully render markdown through the existing qualified path, but the new public template PUT/GET/DELETE surface returns not found for that same qualified collection id. This breaks integration with Axon's namespace-aware collection model.
Suggested fix: Replace the bare-name existence check with namespace-aware resolution such as collection_registered_in_namespace or resolve_collection_key, and add handler or gateway coverage for template CRUD on a registered qualified collection identifier.
title: integration: template CRUD rejects qualified collection identifiers

acceptance: The Postgres-backed axon-storage tests provision their database from the test harness itself, and automated verification of those tests no longer requires .github/workflows/ci.yml to define a postgres service or AXON_TEST_POSTGRES job env for the tests to run.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:535
Related file: .github/workflows/ci.yml:23
Category: drift (rust-cargo)
Severity: medium
Description: The new Postgres regression coverage is enforced only by GitHub Actions job wiring: the test job provisions a postgres service and injects AXON_TEST_POSTGRES in .github/workflows/ci.yml, while the crate-side test code panics whenever CI is set but that workflow-specific environment is absent. I verified the coupling locally with CI=1 cargo test -p axon-storage postgres_roundtrip_when_available, which now fails immediately at crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:539. Active rust-cargo practices require tests needing real external services to use testcontainers, but this implementation keeps the backend provisioning outside the crate and tied to one CI workflow. That makes the regression test non-portable across automated environments and leaves the test contract implicit in workflow configuration rather than the test harness.
Suggested fix: Move the Postgres-backed tests to a self-contained harness, preferably testcontainers or an equivalent crate-owned provisioner, so the axon-storage tests bring up their own database during automated verification instead of depending on workflow-only services and env wiring.
title: drift: Postgres verification depends on workflow-specific service wiring

acceptance: put_collection_view returns an error when the target collection is not registered. SQLite and Postgres schema constrain collection_views to existing collections. Conformance tests cover the unregistered-collection case across adapters.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs:70
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The new collection_views storage is not constrained to registered collections. The SQLite and Postgres DDL create collection_views without a foreign key to collections, and the in-memory adapter's put_collection_view path also writes without checking that the collection is registered. That allows orphan views to be persisted for never-created collections, which breaks the feature spec's collection association invariant and makes cleanup bugs harder to prevent.
Suggested fix: Enforce collection existence before storing a view in every adapter, add SQL foreign keys where applicable, and add conformance tests asserting put_collection_view fails for an unregistered collection.
title: correctness: collection views can be stored for collections that do not exist

acceptance: gRPC version_conflict status detail includes current_entity JSON; cargo test includes a gRPC test asserting current_entity is present in conflict responses
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-server/src/service.rs:38-41
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Spec: FEAT-004, FEAT-008
Description: The gRPC axon_to_status function silently drops the current_entity field from ConflictingVersion errors via the .. pattern. FEAT-004 (US-010 AC4) and FEAT-008 (US-021 AC2) both require that conflict responses include the current entity state so callers can merge and retry. The HTTP gateway correctly serializes current_entity in the 409 JSON body, but the gRPC response only includes expected/actual version numbers. This violates principle P6 (API parity between embedded and server modes).
Suggested fix: Add a current_entity field to the protobuf ConflictingVersion / error response message, serialize the entity into it in axon_to_status, and add a gRPC integration test verifying the field is present.
title: correctness: gRPC conflict response missing current_entity

acceptance: Test exists: begin_tx, put_schema, abort_tx leaves schemas unchanged; abort_tx restores schemas to pre-transaction state
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-storage/src/memory.rs:126-147
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: begin_tx() snapshots self.data but not self.schemas. If a transaction calls put_schema then abort_tx(), entity changes are rolled back but the schema change persists. This violates Principle P4 (Atomicity: all operations commit or none do).
Suggested fix: Include self.schemas in the transaction snapshot alongside self.data. Either create a combined snapshot struct or snapshot both HashMaps.
title: correctness: schema changes bypass MemoryStorageAdapter transaction rollback

acceptance: After rendering a qualified collection, drop_namespace(force=true) and drop_database(force=true) both clear any cached compiled template for removed collections; recreating the same qualified collection with a new template renders the new template, not the stale one; regression tests cover both bulk-drop paths.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:1581
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:1633
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Description: The new compiled-markdown cache is invalidated for drop_collection, but drop_namespace and drop_database delete collections in bulk without clearing the corresponding cache entries. All storage adapters remove collection views during those bulk drops, so the handler can retain compiled templates for collections that no longer exist. If the same qualified collection is recreated later, the new collection view starts back at version 1 and compiled_markdown_template() will happily reuse the stale cached template because the cache key is still the same CollectionId and the version matches again. That returns incorrect markdown after a valid namespace/database drop-and-recreate flow.
Suggested fix: Invalidate cached templates for every collection removed by drop_namespace and drop_database (or clear matching cache entries before/after the storage drop), and add regression tests that render, drop the namespace/database, recreate the same qualified collection, and verify the old template is not reused.
title: integration: bulk namespace/database drops leave stale markdown cache entries

acceptance: delete_entity does not call range_scan with unbounded parameters on the links collection; inbound link check uses a targeted query or index
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:158
Category: performance
Severity: medium
Description: delete_entity performs range_scan(&links_col, None, None, None) which loads ALL link entities into memory to check for inbound references. This is O(N) in total link count per delete call. As link volume grows, every entity delete becomes increasingly expensive.
Suggested fix: Add an indexed query or filtered scan to the StorageAdapter trait that supports querying links by target (collection, id), avoiding the full table scan. Alternatively, maintain a reverse-index of inbound links.
title: performance: delete_entity full table scan for inbound link check

acceptance: handle_put_schema calls self.put_schema() for persistence; compile_entity_schema appears in exactly one method (put_schema); all existing tests pass
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:526-528
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: handle_put_schema (line 526-528) and put_schema (line 54-56) both independently call compile_entity_schema(). handle_put_schema bypasses put_schema() entirely, calling self.storage.put_schema() directly at line 530. Two code paths must be kept in sync for schema validation logic. If validation is extended, one path could be updated without the other, creating a silent validation bypass.
Suggested fix: Refactor handle_put_schema to delegate schema persistence to self.put_schema() instead of self.storage.put_schema() directly. This requires cloning the schema before the call so it remains available for the audit entry and response. The validation in handle_put_schema (lines 526-528) can then be removed.
title: correctness: handle_put_schema duplicates validation logic from put_schema

acceptance: MutationType has LinkCreate and LinkDelete variants; create_link produces a link.create audit entry; delete_link produces a link.delete audit entry; tests verify audit entries via query_audit after each link operation; cargo test -p axon-api -p axon-audit passes
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
Spec: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:448-492
Category: correctness
Severity: high
Description: The new delete_link handler mutates storage (deletes from __axon_links__ and __axon_links_rev__) but never calls self.audit.append(). This violates principle P2 (100% mutation coverage) and P3 (entity and link mutations produce identical audit trail quality). The actor field on DeleteLinkRequest is accepted but silently discarded. Additionally, MutationType lacks LinkCreate/LinkDelete variants. Pre-existing: create_link (line ~400-441) also has no audit entry — the new code followed a bad pattern.
Suggested fix: (1) Add LinkCreate and LinkDelete variants to MutationType in axon-audit/src/entry.rs with Display strings link.create and link.delete. (2) Add self.audit.append() in delete_link recording the link details as before state. (3) Add self.audit.append() in create_link recording the link as after state. (4) Add tests asserting audit entries exist after link create and delete operations.
title: correctness: delete_link (and create_link) missing audit trail entries

description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review of commit 7d905a7.
File: docs/helix/01-frame/prd.md:314,316,400
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The PRD Primary Flow section (lines 314-316) uses 'document' as an Axon data model term: 'bead document' (line 314) and 'matching documents' (line 316). Line 400 says 'storing and querying documents'. Per the terminology decision (axon-1c6adf26), the Axon data model term is 'entity', not 'document'. The PRD is the highest-authority spec, so stale terminology here is more impactful than in feature specs. Note: other PRD uses of 'document' (document stores, document management, PRD is a living document) correctly refer to the English word and should NOT be changed.
Suggested fix: Line 314: 'bead document' -> 'bead entity'. Line 316: 'matching documents' -> 'matching entities'. Line 400: 'querying documents' -> 'querying entities'.
title: correctness: PRD still uses 'document' as Axon data model term in Primary Flow

acceptance: The markdown compiled-template cache has an explicit capacity bound; rendering more unique collections than that bound does not increase the stored entry count past the limit; evicted collections are recompiled on demand and still render correctly; regression tests cover the bounded-cache behavior.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:53
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:115
Category: performance
Severity: medium
Description: FEAT-026's non-functional requirements explicitly say the template cache must be bounded, but the new MarkdownTemplateCache is a plain HashMap with no size limit or eviction policy. Every first render of a new collection inserts another compiled template that lives until process restart, so a long-lived server can grow memory monotonically with the number of collections it has ever rendered. Removed collections also leave stale entries behind unless a specific invalidation path happens to clear them.
Suggested fix: Replace the raw HashMap with a bounded cache (for example an LRU keyed by CollectionId with an explicit capacity), enforce the limit on insert, and add regression coverage that proves rendering more than the configured capacity does not let the cache grow without bound while still returning correct markdown.
title: performance: markdown template cache is unbounded

acceptance: Attempting to drop the implicit default database either returns a deterministic error or leaves the instance with a recreated `default.default` namespace; unqualified collection creation still works after the operation; `/health` reports the real default-namespace status from storage; an HTTP integration test covers the chosen behavior
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:1157-1158
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:1519-1550
File: crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs:1042-1054
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Description: The new database drop path allows `default` to be removed, but the rest of the system still hardcodes unqualified collection work to `Namespace::default_ns()` and the HTTP health endpoint always reports `"default_namespace": "default.default"`. After `DELETE /databases/default?force=true`, zero-config collection creation and any other default-scoped paths fail because the backing catalog no longer contains `default.default`, while `/health` continues to claim that the default namespace exists and is healthy. That violates FEAT-014's zero-config invariant and makes the new health signal misleading for operators or future control-plane automation.
Suggested fix: Either forbid dropping the implicit `default` database/schema or recreate them immediately after a forced drop, and derive the health payload from actual storage state instead of hardcoding `default.default`. Add an integration test that exercises the default-database drop path and verifies both collection behavior and `/health` reporting.
title: integration: dropping default database breaks zero-config paths and health still advertises it

description:
Learning from fresh-eyes review of commit 89314f0.

AuditLog::append is immediate and non-transactional. When Transaction::commit calls execute() and then either commit_tx() fails or execute() itself fails partway through, the storage-level transaction is rolled back via abort_tx(), but audit entries written during execute() persist.

This means the audit log can contain entries for mutations that were never committed. These entries have transaction_id set, but there is no status field to distinguish committed from rolled-back entries.

Evidence:
- AuditLog trait (crates/axon-audit/src/log.rs): append() is immediate, no rollback capability.
- Transaction::commit (crates/axon-api/src/transaction.rs:132-157): execute() writes audit entries before commit_tx is called.
- MemoryStorageAdapter::abort_tx restores snapshot, but audit log is separate.

Options to consider:
1. Buffer audit entries in Transaction and only append after commit_tx succeeds.
2. Add a status/outcome field to audit entries (committed vs rolled-back).
3. Accept as intentional and document the behavior.
title: design: audit log entries persist after transaction rollback

acceptance: Template PUT and DELETE append durable audit entries with actor, timestamp, and before/after template state; the audit log exposes distinct template mutation records; handler or gateway tests verify audited create/update/delete template flows.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:1505
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:1564
File: crates/axon-audit/src/entry.rs:17
File: docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-026-markdown-templates.md:322
Category: correctness
Severity: high
Description: The new public template PUT/GET/DELETE surfaces persist and delete collection views directly, but neither put_collection_template nor delete_collection_template appends an audit entry, and the audit mutation enum has no template-specific operation at all. FEAT-026 explicitly requires template changes to be audited with actor, timestamp, and before/after state. As shipped, operators can change or remove a collection's presentation layer without any durable audit trail, violating both the feature contract and Axon's audit-first architecture.
Suggested fix: Introduce audit mutation types for template create/update/delete (or an equivalent collection-view mutation), capture before/after template state, and append audit entries from the handler on successful template writes and deletes. Add handler and gateway tests that query the audit log and verify actor plus before/after metadata for template changes.
title: correctness: template CRUD writes are not audited

acceptance: SQLite stores and reads schema/version/view metadata under the same resolved qualified collection identity used for entities; qualified writes followed by bare reads on a uniquely resolvable namespaced collection succeed for schema and view APIs; regression tests cover the affected schema and collection-view methods.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs:1085
File: crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs:1113
File: crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs:1130
File: crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs:1138
File: crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs:1245
File: crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs:1280
File: crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs:1301
File: crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs:1308
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The SQLite entity table fix resolves qualified collection identity to (database_name, schema_name, bare collection), but schema and collection-view persistence still use the caller's raw collection string in both inserts and reads. A qualified write such as put_schema(prod.billing.invoices) stores collection = prod.billing.invoices, while a later bare lookup that resolves uniquely to the same namespace queries for collection = invoices and misses. The same mismatch exists for collection views. That makes metadata access inconsistent with entity CRUD and breaks any flow that relies on bare-name resolution after registering a unique non-default namespace.
Suggested fix: Normalize schema-version and collection-view SQL to use the resolved bare collection key consistently, just like entity CRUD does. Add regression tests covering qualified writes followed by bare reads for get_schema, get_schema_version, list_schema_versions, and get_collection_view on a uniquely resolvable namespaced collection.
title: correctness: sqlite schema and view lookups key off the raw collection string

acceptance: After force-dropping a namespace or database, no outbound or inbound link query returns links whose source or target belongs to a removed collection, unrelated links remain intact, and regression tests cover the namespace/database drop paths.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:1500
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:1552
File: crates/axon-storage/src/adapter.rs:572
File: crates/axon-storage/src/memory.rs:857
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The new namespace/database force-drop flow purges entity rows and appends collection-drop audits, but it never removes links whose source or target belongs to the dropped collections. In the default storage path, links are stored as separate pseudo-collection entities (__axon_links__ / __axon_links_rev__), and the in-memory adapter also keeps them in a dedicated link map. None of those stores are touched by drop_namespace/drop_database, so link queries can still return edges to deleted entities after a forced drop, violating FEAT-014's 'all contents' requirement and the technical requirement that link integrity hold.
Suggested fix: Cascade force-drop through link storage for every removed collection (forward and reverse link entries, plus the in-memory dedicated link map), then add regression tests showing namespace/database drop removes links attached to deleted collections while preserving unrelated links.
title: correctness: force-drop leaves dangling link records for removed collections

acceptance: ADR-004 Protocol section commit sequence shows audit entries flushed after commit_tx(), not before. grep for 'Write all audit entries' in the Protocol block returns 0 or the line clearly indicates post-commit timing.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review of commit 5b749c1.
File: docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-004-transaction-model.md:53-55
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: ADR-004 Protocol section (lines 53-55) shows the commit sequence as: (1) Apply all entity writes, (2) Write all audit entries, (3) Commit storage transaction. This implies audit entries are written before commit_tx(), inside the storage transaction. However, ADR-004's own Audit Integration section (lines 93-94) correctly states audit entries are buffered and flushed only after commit_tx() succeeds. The code (transaction.rs:152-160) confirms post-commit flush. This is the same class of residual same-transaction audit claim that commit 5b749c1 fixed in ADR-003.
Suggested fix: Update the Protocol commit sequence to show audit entry buffering during execute, storage commit, then post-commit audit flush. E.g.: Apply all entity writes (version + 1) -> Commit storage transaction (atomic via StorageAdapter) -> Flush buffered audit entries (post-commit).
title: correctness: ADR-004 Protocol section describes pre-commit audit writes, contradicting post-commit strategy

acceptance: When `get_entity_markdown` returns `RenderFailed`, `axon --output json entity get ... --render markdown` and `axon --output yaml entity get ... --render markdown` emit parseable structured output containing both the entity payload and the render-failure detail; regression tests cover both formats.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-cli/src/main.rs:891
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The new markdown CLI path now serializes `GetEntityMarkdownResponse` for JSON/YAML only when the handler returns `Rendered`, but the adjacent `RenderFailed` arm still bails immediately. That throws away the structured failure payload even though `AxonHandler::get_entity_markdown()` deliberately preserves the fetched entity in `RenderFailed { entity, detail }` and the HTTP gateway surfaces that payload on markdown render failure. In `--output json` or `--output yaml` mode, scripts and agents therefore still lose the machine-readable entity fallback on the error path, which is exactly when structured output is most valuable.
Suggested fix: In JSON/YAML modes, serialize the full `GetEntityMarkdownResponse` for both `Rendered` and `RenderFailed` outcomes, then return a non-zero exit status if desired after writing the structured payload. Add CLI regression coverage for render-failure JSON and YAML output so the entity payload and detail remain available to machine consumers.
title: correctness: CLI markdown structured output drops render-failure payload

acceptance: On an upgraded Postgres catalog where legacy schema or collection-view rows still store the raw qualified collection string and default namespace columns, `unregister_collection(<qualified>)` removes those rows completely; a regression test seeds that state for both tables and verifies zero matching metadata rows remain afterward.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:215
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:227
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:1184
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:1210
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The fresh fix deletes raw-qualified legacy metadata rows only inside the resolved namespace (`database_name = prod`, `schema_name = billing`), but the upgrade path that created this class of legacy rows can leave them in `default/default`. `ensure_namespace_catalog_tables()` only backfills namespace columns by joining `schemas.collection = collections.name` and `collection_views.collection = collections.name`. For pre-fix rows whose `collection` value is still the raw qualified identifier like `prod.billing.invoices`, those joins miss and the newly added namespace columns stay at their default values. A later `unregister_collection(prod.billing.invoices)` now deletes the normalized bare rows in `prod.billing`, plus raw-qualified rows in `prod.billing`, but it still does not delete the legacy raw-qualified rows stranded in `default/default`. The new regression test seeds the legacy row directly in the resolved namespace, so it does not cover the actual upgrade state implied by the migration SQL.
Suggested fix: Normalize or clean up raw-qualified legacy rows using the parsed collection identifier rather than only the resolved namespace. Either rewrite legacy `schemas` and `collection_views` rows during upgrade so raw qualified identifiers get their correct `database_name` and `schema_name`, or make `unregister_collection` also delete raw-qualified legacy rows that still sit in `default/default`. Add a regression test that seeds a pre-upgrade raw-qualified schema and collection-view row with default namespace columns and verifies qualified unregister removes them fully.
title: correctness: default-namespaced legacy Postgres metadata survives qualified unregister

Category: correctness

acceptance: filter_depth uses an iterative (non-recursive) algorithm. Test: a FilterNode tree of depth 100_000 returns the correct depth value without stack overflow.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:686-692
Spec: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs
Category: security/correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The filter_depth() function uses recursion to measure FilterNode tree depth. If an attacker constructs a tree deep enough to overflow the stack (e.g. millions of levels via a non-JSON path), the overflow occurs inside filter_depth before the depth check at line 228 can reject it. The commit message for 9d63b5a claims 'safe linear traversal' but the implementation recurses. Practical risk is mitigated by serde_json's default recursion limit (128) for HTTP deserialization, and 1000 levels works in tests. But defense-in-depth is incomplete — the guard itself shares the vulnerability it protects against.
Suggested fix: Rewrite filter_depth as an iterative traversal using an explicit Vec<&FilterNode> stack, eliminating recursive calls.
title: security: filter_depth itself uses unbounded recursion

acceptance: 1. Transaction::commit calls abort_tx() when commit_tx() fails. 2. A test exists that simulates commit_tx failure and verifies abort_tx is called and the adapter is not wedged.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/transaction.rs:141-143
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Spec: crates/axon-api/src/transaction.rs
Description: In Transaction::commit, when execute() succeeds but commit_tx() fails, no abort_tx() is called. The storage-level transaction is left open with uncommitted writes buffered. For SqliteStorageAdapter, in_tx stays true, wedging the adapter so subsequent begin_tx() calls are rejected. This violates P4 (Transactions Mean Transactions) — a failed commit should leave the system in a clean state.
Suggested fix: Wrap commit_tx() failure to call abort_tx() before propagating the error:
  Ok(written) => match storage.commit_tx() {
      Ok(()) => Ok(written),
      Err(e) => { let _ = storage.abort_tx(); Err(e) }
  }
title: correctness: abort_tx not called on commit_tx failure in Transaction::commit

Description: FEAT-014 and ADR-011 define two-part collection names as schema-qualified shorthand using the current database, so billing.invoices must resolve to {current_db}.billing.invoices (in the default case, default.billing.invoices). Namespace::parse still interprets every two-part name as database.collection and returns (billing.default, invoices), and the unit test at parse_two_part_name locks that behavior in. Because resolve_collection_key and all adapter resolve_catalog_key implementations call Namespace::parse, the reviewed OCC fix only preserves identity for three-part names; a two-part update/patch/get path will route to the wrong namespace, and if a database named billing exists it can silently target the wrong tenant.
Suggested fix: Change two-part parsing to mean schema.collection with the current/default database applied, update the affected unit tests, and add handler/storage regression coverage proving billing.invoices resolves to default.billing.invoices in default-db flows while three-part names keep working unchanged.
title: integration: two-part collection names resolve as database.collection instead of schema.collection

Description: Postgres now keys entity rows by (database_name, schema_name, bare collection, id), but the schema and collection-view tables still persist and query the caller's raw collection string. A qualified write such as put_schema(prod.billing.invoices) stores collection = prod.billing.invoices, while a later bare lookup that resolves uniquely to the same namespace queries for collection = invoices and returns nothing. The same mismatch applies to collection views. This leaves metadata behavior out of sync with the entity data path and breaks bare-name access once a unique non-default namespace is involved.
Suggested fix: Normalize Postgres schema and collection-view persistence/lookups to use the resolved bare collection key consistently, matching the entity CRUD path. Add regression tests for qualified writes followed by bare reads on schema/version/view methods for a uniquely resolvable namespaced collection.

acceptance: Opening a SQLite database created with the pre-fix collection_views schema and then unregistering a collection removes any stored collection view. An automated regression test covers the SQLite upgrade path.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs:53
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Description: The SQLite fix only adds the `collection_views -> collections` foreign key inside `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS`. On databases created before commit f3bfb06, `collection_views` already exists without that constraint, so opening the database with the new code does not backfill the foreign key. `unregister_collection()` still deletes only from `collections`, which leaves stale collection views behind on upgraded databases. I reproduced this by creating the pre-fix schema, running the new init SQL, inserting a collection and view, deleting the collection, and observing `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM collection_views` still returned `1`.
Suggested fix: Add an explicit SQLite migration or fallback cleanup path for upgraded databases. At minimum, `unregister_collection()` should delete from `collection_views` directly; ideally the adapter should also migrate existing SQLite databases so the foreign key and cascade are present after upgrade, and add an upgrade regression test.
title: integration: SQLite upgrades keep stale collection views after unregister

acceptance: The GraphQL helper-contract probe no longer requests the full __schema.types table; a regression test verifies the probe only asks for the specific root fields and named helper types it validates, and the existing helper-contract tests still pass
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: ui/src/lib/graphql.ts:13
Category: performance
Severity: medium
Description: The new helper-contract probe now requests the full __schema.types table for the entire GraphQL schema before the first UI data fetch. That is much broader than the helper needs: the code only validates three root fields plus the shapes of CollectionMeta, EntityConnection, EntityEdge, PageInfo, and the entity return type. On non-trivial schemas this turns every cold helper call into a full-schema introspection round trip, increasing startup latency and response size well beyond the previous targeted probe. It also works against FEAT-015 US-049's introspection latency goal and risks tripping future GraphQL depth or complexity limits once those protections are enabled.
Suggested fix: Replace the full schema type scan with a targeted contract probe. For example, inspect the query root first and then fetch only the named types referenced by the helper fields, or split the validation into smaller __type(name: ...) queries so the client never downloads the entire schema just to decide whether these helpers are safe to use.

description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:390
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:399
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:410

File: crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs:824
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:568
Category: correctness
Severity: high
Description: compare_and_swap now canonicalizes the stored entity back to the bare collection name, and update_entity/patch_entity reuse that bare name for post-write side effects and the API response. In a FEAT-014 setup where prod.billing.invoices and prod.engineering.invoices both exist, indexed or gated updates patch the entity first and then call update_indexes, update_compound_indexes, put_gate_results, and audit logging with bare invoices. Those storage helpers resolve collection identity again and reject the ambiguous bare name, so the handler can return an error after the entity row has already been updated, leaving indexes, gate materialization, and audit inconsistent with persisted data. Even when no secondary maintenance runs, the update/patch response and audit entry lose the qualified collection identity.
Suggested fix: Preserve the qualified/requested collection identity across OCC update paths. Either have storage return the qualified collection it resolved, or have the handler keep using req.collection for every post-write side effect, audit entry, and response presentation. Add regression tests that update and patch indexed or gated entities in two same-named namespaces through the handler/API and prove the operation succeeds atomically with qualified collection identity preserved end to end.
title: correctness: namespaced update and patch reuse bare collection after OCC

acceptance: Either: (1) audit entries are written within the StorageAdapter transaction before commit_tx(), verified by a test that crashes between commit and audit-append and shows no gap; OR (2) ADR-003 and ADR-004 explicitly document the post-commit audit strategy with a documented recovery mechanism, and INV-003 audit completeness invariant accounts for the gap
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-004-transaction-model.md:91-92
File: crates/axon-api/src/transaction.rs:144-151
Category: correctness
Severity: high
Description: ADR-004 (line 91-92) and ADR-003 (audit write path diagram) both specify that audit entries are written in the same storage transaction as entity mutations, ensuring atomic commit/rollback. However, transaction.rs writes audit entries AFTER commit_tx() succeeds (line 144-151). The code comment explicitly says 'The storage transaction is already durable at this point, so audit failures do not undo the committed data.' This creates a crash-safety gap: if the process dies between commit_tx() and audit append, committed mutations have no audit trail. This contradicts principle P2 (Audit is Not Optional - 100% mutation coverage).
Suggested fix: Either (a) refactor the AuditLog to write through the StorageAdapter transaction handle so audit entries are part of the same storage transaction, or (b) update ADR-003 and ADR-004 to document the post-commit audit strategy and add a recovery mechanism (e.g., write-ahead intent log or audit reconciliation on startup).
title: correctness: audit entries written post-commit contradicts ADR-003/ADR-004 same-transaction guarantee

description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:525-526
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: create_collection calls register_collection (line 525) then put_schema (line 526) sequentially. If put_schema fails (e.g. invalid entity_schema that fails compile_entity_schema), the collection is left registered but schemaless. This violates the documented invariant 'schemaless collections are not supported' (FEAT-001). A subsequent create_collection call for the same name would fail with AlreadyExists. The user would need to drop_collection and retry. The same atomicity gap exists with the audit append on line 528 — a schema+collection could be persisted with no audit trail.
Suggested fix: Either (a) validate the schema before calling register_collection (move put_schema or at least compile_entity_schema before the register call), or (b) roll back register_collection on put_schema failure. Option (a) is simpler and sufficient since validation is the likely failure mode.

acceptance: Memory, SQLite, and Postgres adapters can register `invoices` in both `prod.billing` and `prod.engineering` without collision; listing each namespace returns only its own collection; dropping one namespace or database does not remove or corrupt the same-named collection in another namespace; automated regression tests cover all three adapters
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-core/src/id.rs:74-90
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:1153-1159
File: crates/axon-storage/src/memory.rs:497-501
File: crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs:80-87
File: crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs:827-833
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:67-75
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:685-689
Category: correctness
Severity: high
Description: FEAT-014 requires collection names to be unique within a schema so `prod.billing.invoices` and `prod.engineering.invoices` can coexist. The new catalog persistence still keys collection identity by the bare `CollectionId` string everywhere. `CollectionId` has no namespace component, `create_collection` still rejects any name already present in the global `list_collections()` set and always registers into `default.default`, MemoryStorageAdapter maps `collection_namespaces` by bare collection ID, and the durable adapters keep `collections.name` as the primary key and `collection_views` keyed only by that name. As a result, the second same-named collection across namespaces is either rejected or silently collapsed onto the first one, so namespace isolation and force-drop semantics cannot be correct once real namespace-scoped collection creation lands.
Suggested fix: Thread namespace-qualified collection identity through the core model and storage adapters. At minimum, make collection catalog keys unique on `(database_name, schema_name, name)`, update `CollectionId` or add a distinct fully-qualified identifier for namespace-aware paths, and add regression tests proving the same collection name can exist independently in multiple namespaces across Memory, SQLite, and Postgres.
title: correctness: namespace catalogs still collapse collections by bare name

Category: drift (rust-cargo)
Severity: medium
Description: The two PostgreSQL-backed tests are now unconditional members of the default `cargo test -p axon-storage` suite. When `AXON_TEST_POSTGRES` is unset and a container runtime is unavailable, the tests panic during setup instead of skipping or being moved behind an explicit integration gate. Suggested fix: Gate the Postgres-backed tests so the default `cargo test -p axon-storage` suite remains green without Docker or `AXON_TEST_POSTGRES` (for example by skipping with a clear message when neither is available, or by moving them behind an explicit integration feature/ignored test target) while preserving an explicit path that exercises the Postgres adapter in automated environments.

acceptance: axon-render exposes a reusable compiled-template API; the markdown GET path reuses compiled templates per collection instead of reparsing on every request; a test covers cache invalidation after template update
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-render/src/lib.rs:260
Category: performance
Severity: high
Description: `axon_render::render` calls `Template::new(template)` on every render. FEAT-026 requires compiled templates to be cached per collection and the documented handler flow says `axon-api` should call `axon_render::render`. With the current public API, downstream code cannot satisfy the per-collection cache requirement without bypassing the crate abstraction and reparsing the template on every request.
Suggested fix: Introduce a reusable compiled-template API in `axon-render` (for example a wrapper type around `ramhorns::Template` or a `compile()` + `render_compiled()` pair) and update the GET render path to cache compiled templates per collection, invalidating that cache on template updates.
title: performance: render() recompiles templates on every call

acceptance: DELETE /collections/{collection}/template succeeds when the request has Content-Type: application/json and an empty body; a gateway regression test covers that request shape; cargo test -p axon-server http_collection_template_crud_round_trip_uses_public_surface passes with the new regression test also green
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs:813
File: /home/erik/.cargo/registry/src/index.crates.io-1949cf8c6b5b557f/axum-0.8.8/src/json.rs:123
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Description: The new DELETE template handler switched from a body-less route to Option<Json<DeleteCollectionTemplateBody>>. Under the axum 0.8.8 OptionalFromRequest implementation, Option<Json<_>> is only optional when the Content-Type header is absent; if a client sends Content-Type: application/json, axum always tries to deserialize the body and rejects empty bodies with a JSON syntax error. That means generic HTTP clients that send an empty JSON DELETE now get a 4xx response even though the route previously succeeded and the body is semantically optional.
Suggested fix: Replace the extractor with a tolerant parser that treats an empty JSON body as no actor, or accept raw bytes and parse an actor object only when a non-empty body is present. Add a gateway regression test that sends DELETE /collections/{collection}/template with Content-Type: application/json and an empty body and asserts success.
title: integration: template DELETE rejects empty JSON requests
